infobox.value: ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): ゴールドシップ, [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"): *Gōrudo Shippu*; foaled 6 March 2009) is a retired Japanese [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") racehorse. In a racing career which began in July 2011, he ran twenty-eight times and won thirteen races. As a two-year-old in 2011, he won two of his four races. In 2012, he won the [Satsuki Shō](/wiki/Satsuki_Sh%C5%8D "Satsuki Shō") and the [Kikuka Shō](/wiki/Kikuka_Sh%C5%8D "Kikuka Shō"), the first and third legs of the [Japanese Triple Crown](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing#Japan "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing"). In December, he defeated a strong field in the [weight-for-age](/wiki/Weight-for-age "Weight-for-age") invitational [Arima Kinen](/wiki/Arima_Kinen "Arima Kinen"), which led to his being rated the best three-year-old racehorse in the world by some authorities. As a four-year-old, he ran disappointingly in the [Tennō Shō](/wiki/Tenn%C5%8D_Sh%C5%8D "Tennō Shō") but returned to form to win the [Takarazuka Kinen](/wiki/Takarazuka_Kinen "Takarazuka Kinen"). As a five-year-old, Gold Ship became the first horse to record a second victory in the Takarazuka Kinen and in 2015 he added a win in the Tennō Shō. He is known for his unpredictable temperament, with major wins being interspersed with inexplicably poor efforts.

**Gold Ship** ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): ゴールドシップ, [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"): *Gōrudo Shippu*; foaled 6 March 2009) is a retired Japanese [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") racehorse. In a racing career which began in July 2011, he ran twenty-eight times and won thirteen races. As a two-year-old in 2011, he won two of his four races. In 2012, he won the [Satsuki Shō](/wiki/Satsuki_Sh%C5%8D "Satsuki Shō") and the [Kikuka Shō](/wiki/Kikuka_Sh%C5%8D "Kikuka Shō"), the first and third legs of the [Japanese Triple Crown](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing#Japan "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing"). In December, he defeated a strong field in the [weight-for-age](/wiki/Weight-for-age "Weight-for-age") invitational [Arima Kinen](/wiki/Arima_Kinen "Arima Kinen"), which led to his being rated the best three-year-old racehorse in the world by some authorities. As a four-year-old, he ran disappointingly in the [Tennō Shō](/wiki/Tenn%C5%8D_Sh%C5%8D "Tennō Shō") but returned to form to win the [Takarazuka Kinen](/wiki/Takarazuka_Kinen "Takarazuka Kinen"). As a five-year-old, Gold Ship became the first horse to record a second victory in the Takarazuka Kinen and in 2015 he added a win in the Tennō Shō. He is known for his unpredictable temperament, with major wins being interspersed with inexplicably poor efforts.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Ship&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Gold Ship is a light-coloured grey stallion bred in Japan by his owner Eiichi Kobayashi. His sire, [Stay Gold](/wiki/Stay_Gold_(horse) "Stay Gold (horse)"), a son of the thirteen-time [leading sire in Japan](/wiki/Leading_sire_in_Japan "Leading sire in Japan") [Sunday Silence](/wiki/Sunday_Silence "Sunday Silence"), was a successful international performer, winning the [Dubai Sheema Classic](/wiki/Dubai_Sheema_Classic "Dubai Sheema Classic") and the [Hong Kong Vase](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Vase "Hong Kong Vase"). Standing at stud at the Big Red Farm in [Hokkaido](/wiki/Hokkaido "Hokkaido"), he produced numerous important winners including [Dream Journey](/wiki/Dream_Journey "Dream Journey"), [Orfevre](/wiki/Orfevre "Orfevre"), [Nakayama Festa](/wiki/Nakayama_Festa "Nakayama Festa"), and, [Fenomeno](/wiki/Fenomeno_(horse) "Fenomeno (horse)").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Gold Ship's dam, Point Flag, from whom he [inherited his grey coat](/wiki/Gray_(horse)#The_genetics_of_gray "Gray (horse)"), was a daughter of the [Hall of Fame](/wiki/Japan_Racing_Association_Hall_of_Fame "Japan Racing Association Hall of Fame") inductee [Mejiro McQueen](/wiki/Mejiro_McQueen "Mejiro McQueen").

Point Flag was a large horse, weighing well over 500 kg during her career, with her previous offspring reaching similar sizes. On the other hand, Stay Gold was much the opposite, short and light, being only 161 cm in height and under 450 kg during his racing career, but had a much stronger and more resilient body.[[2]](#cite_note-2) By pairing the two, it had been hoped by the breeders at Deguchi Bokujo farm that it would result in a horse that would be more average sized and therefore less prone to the same leg problems that Point Flag had faced due to her size. The experiment was a failure; while Gold Ship inherited his sire's hardy constitution, he also inherited his dam's size; even when young, Gold Ship was big enough to stand out amongst the other horses his age.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Racing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Ship&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Racing career")]

### 2011: two-year-old season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Ship&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2011: two-year-old season")]

Gold Ship began his racing career in Hokkaido by winning a maiden race at [Hakodate Racecourse](/wiki/Hakodate_Racecourse "Hakodate Racecourse") on 9 July and followed up with a win in the nine-[furlong](/wiki/Furlong "Furlong") Cosmos Shō at [Sapporo Racecourse](/wiki/Sapporo_Racecourse "Sapporo Racecourse") on 10 September.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He was then moved up to [Grade Three](/wiki/Group_races "Group races") class to contest the [Sapporo Nisai Stakes](/wiki/Sapporo_Nisai_Stakes "Sapporo Nisai Stakes") over the same course and distance on 1 October. He started at odds of [7/2](/wiki/Fractional_odds "Fractional odds") and finished second, half a [length](/wiki/Horse_length "Horse length") behind the winner, [Grandezza](/w/index.php?title=Grandezza&action=edit&redlink=1 "Grandezza (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%B0%E3%83%A9%E3%83%B3%E3%83%87%E3%83%83%E3%83%84%E3%82%A1 "ja:グランデッツァ")]. On his only other start of the season, he finished second to [Adam's Peak](/w/index.php?title=Adam%27s_Peak_(horse)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Adam's Peak (horse) (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%A2%E3%83%80%E3%83%A0%E3%82%B9%E3%83%94%E3%83%BC%E3%82%AF_(%E7%AB%B6%E8%B5%B0%E9%A6%AC) "ja:アダムスピーク (競走馬)")] in the Grade Three [Radio Nikkei Hai Nisai Stakes](/wiki/Hopeful_Stakes_(Japan) "Hopeful Stakes (Japan)") over ten furlongs at [Hanshin Racecourse](/wiki/Hanshin_Racecourse "Hanshin Racecourse") on 24 December.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### 2012: three-year-old season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Ship&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2012: three-year-old season")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/%E3%82%B4%E3%83%BC%E3%83%AB%E3%83%89%E3%82%B7%E3%83%83%E3%83%97.JPG/250px-%E3%82%B4%E3%83%BC%E3%83%AB%E3%83%89%E3%82%B7%E3%83%83%E3%83%97.JPG)](/wiki/File:%E3%82%B4%E3%83%BC%E3%83%AB%E3%83%89%E3%82%B7%E3%83%83%E3%83%97.JPG)

Gold Ship after winning the 2012 Arima Kinen

On his first appearance as a three-year-old, Gold Ship contested the Grade Three [Kyodo Tsushin Hai](/wiki/Kyodo_Tsushin_Hai "Kyodo Tsushin Hai") at [Tokyo Racecourse](/wiki/Tokyo_Racecourse "Tokyo Racecourse") in February. Ridden for the first time by [Hiroyuki Uchida](/w/index.php?title=Hiroyuki_Uchida&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hiroyuki Uchida (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%86%85%E7%94%B0%E5%8D%9A%E5%B9%B8 "ja:内田博幸")], he overtook the leader, [Deep Brillante](/wiki/Deep_Brillante "Deep Brillante"), inside the final furlong to record his first win by one and three quarter lengths.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In April at [Nakayama Racecourse](/wiki/Nakayama_Racecourse "Nakayama Racecourse") Gold Ship started at odds of 6.1/1 for the [Satsuki Shō](/wiki/Satsuki_Sh%C5%8D "Satsuki Shō"), the first leg of the [Japanese Triple Crown](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing#Japan "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing"). Apparently relishing the extremely wet conditions,[[7]](#cite_note-7) he came from last place to take the lead in the straight and won by two and a half lengths from [World Ace](/w/index.php?title=World_Ace&action=edit&redlink=1 "World Ace (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%AF%E3%83%BC%E3%83%AB%E3%83%89%E3%82%A8%E3%83%BC%E3%82%B9 "ja:ワールドエース")] and Deep Brilante, with Grandezza and Adam's Peak unplaced.[[8]](#cite_note-8) For Gold Ship's jockey, Uchida, this Satsuki Shō marked his first victory after recovering from a broken back sustained in a fall in May 2011. Gold Ship was made the 2.1/1 second favourite for the [Tokyo Yushun](/wiki/Tokyo_Yushun "Tokyo Yushun") (Japanese Derby) over one and a half miles and finished fifth, one and a half lengths behind the winner, Deep Brillante.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Gold Ship returned from the summer break in the Grade Two [Kobe Shimbun Hai](/wiki/Kobe_Shimbun_Hai "Kobe Shimbun Hai"), a trial race for the [Kikuka-shō](/wiki/Kikuka-sh%C5%8D "Kikuka-shō") (Japanese St Leger), at Hanshin on 27 September. He started the 1.3/1 favourite and won by two and a half lengths from fifteen opponents. A month later, he started odds-on favourite for the Kikuka-shō over fifteen furlongs at [Kyoto Racecourse](/wiki/Kyoto_Racecourse "Kyoto Racecourse") and won by one and three quarter lengths from Sky Dignity.[[10]](#cite_note-10) As in the Satsuki Shō, the colt came from last place before he "cruised" to victory, although the competition was less strong than anticipated following the late withdrawal of Deep Brillante, who had sustained a tendon injury.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Gold Ship was one of sixteen horses to be invited to contest the all-aged [Arima Kinen](/wiki/Arima_Kinen "Arima Kinen") at Nakayama on 23 December. He was towards the back of the field before moving up on the wide outside on the approach to the straight. In the closing stages, he overtook the leaders and won by one and a half lengths from [Ocean Blue](/w/index.php?title=Ocean_Blue_(horse)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ocean Blue (horse) (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%AA%E3%83%BC%E3%82%B7%E3%83%A3%E3%83%B3%E3%83%96%E3%83%AB%E3%83%BC_(%E7%AB%B6%E8%B5%B0%E9%A6%AC) "ja:オーシャンブルー (競走馬)")], with Uchida standing up in the irons and saluting the crowd as he crossed the line. [Naosuke Sugai](/wiki/Naosuke_Sugai "Naosuke Sugai") said that the horse would stay in Japan in 2013 before challenging for international races such as the [Dubai World Cup](/wiki/Dubai_World_Cup "Dubai World Cup") and [Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe](/wiki/Prix_de_l%27Arc_de_Triomphe "Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe") in 2014, saying that "this is just the beginning for him".[[12]](#cite_note-12) Britain's *[Racing Post](/wiki/Racing_Post "Racing Post")* described the winner's performance as "a stunning display" whilst pointing out that Japan's other two outstanding middle-distance horses, [Orfevre](/wiki/Orfevre "Orfevre") and [Gentildonna](/wiki/Gentildonna "Gentildonna"), had bypassed the race.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### 2013: four-year-old season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Ship&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2013: four-year-old season")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Gold_Ship_Takarazuka_kinen_2013.jpg/250px-Gold_Ship_Takarazuka_kinen_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gold_Ship_Takarazuka_kinen_2013.jpg)

Gold Ship at the 2013 Takarazuka Kinen

Gold Ship began his four-year-old season on 17 March in the Grade Two [Hanshin Daishoten](/wiki/Hanshin_Daishoten "Hanshin Daishoten"), a trial race for the Spring [Tennō Shō](/wiki/Tenn%C5%8D_Sh%C5%8D "Tennō Shō"). Starting at odds of 1/10 he won the fourteen-furlong race by two lengths from [Desperado](/w/index.php?title=Desperado_(horse)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Desperado (horse) (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%87%E3%82%B9%E3%83%9A%E3%83%A9%E3%83%BC%E3%83%89_(%E7%AB%B6%E8%B5%B0%E9%A6%AC) "ja:デスペラード (競走馬)")]. On 28 April, the colt was moved up further in distance for the two-mile Spring Tennō Shō at Kyoto. He started odds-on favourite and finished fifth of the eighteen runners behind [Fenomeno](/wiki/Fenomeno_(horse) "Fenomeno (horse)"). On 23 June, Gold Ship met Fenomeno again in the [Takarazuka Kinen](/wiki/Takarazuka_Kinen "Takarazuka Kinen") at Hanshin Racecourse where the field, which was decided by public ballot, also included Gentildonna. Orfevre was withdrawn from the race after bleeding after an exercise gallop on 14 June. Uchida positioned the grey just behind the early leaders before moving up on the outside in the straight. He hit the front inside the final furlong and drew clear to win by three and a half lengths from the five-year-old [Danon Ballade](/w/index.php?title=Danon_Ballade&action=edit&redlink=1 "Danon Ballade (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%80%E3%83%8E%E3%83%B3%E3%83%90%E3%83%A9%E3%83%BC%E3%83%89 "ja:ダノンバラード")], with Gentildonna third and Fenomeno fourth.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Gold Ship's autumn campaign began in the Grade II [Kyoto Daishoten](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Dto_Daish%C5%8Dten "Kyōto Daishōten") on 2 October. He was made the 1/5 favourite and finished fifth of the thirteen runners behind the 165/1 outsider [HIt The Target](/w/index.php?title=Hit_The_Target_(horse)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hit The Target (horse) (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%92%E3%83%83%E3%83%88%E3%82%B6%E3%82%BF%E3%83%BC%E3%82%B2%E3%83%83%E3%83%88 "ja:ヒットザターゲット")]. Sugai offered several explanations for the colt's poor run, including the unseasonably hot weather, the fast track, and an unfavourable draw.[[15]](#cite_note-15) On 24 November, Gold Ship started second favourite behind Gentildonna for the [Japan Cup](/wiki/Japan_Cup "Japan Cup"). He was never in contention at any stage and finished fifteenth of the seventeen runners. For the horse's last race of the year, the British jockey [Ryan Moore](/wiki/Ryan_Moore_(jockey) "Ryan Moore (jockey)") took over from Uchida in the Arima Kinen. He started at odds of 17/5 and finished third behind [Orfevre](/wiki/Orfevre "Orfevre") and [Win Variation](/w/index.php?title=Win_Variation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Win Variation (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%A6%E3%82%A4%E3%83%B3%E3%83%90%E3%83%AA%E3%82%A2%E3%82%B7%E3%82%AA%E3%83%B3 "ja:ウインバリアシオン")].

### 2014: five-year-old season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Ship&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2014: five-year-old season")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Gold_Ship_Hanshin_Daishoten_2014%28IMG1%29.jpg/250px-Gold_Ship_Hanshin_Daishoten_2014%28IMG1%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gold_Ship_Hanshin_Daishoten_2014(IMG1).jpg)

Gold Ship at the 2014 Hanshin Daishoten

Gold Ship began his fourth season by repeating his 2013 success in the Hanshin Daishoten: he started the 7/10 favourite and won by three and a half lengths from [Admire Rakti](/wiki/Admire_Rakti "Admire Rakti"). On 4 May, Gold Ship started 3.3/1 second favourite for the Tennō Shō but was never near enough to challenge and finished seventh of the eighteen runners behind Fenomeno. On 29 June at Hanshin, he attempted to become the first horse to record back-to-back victories in the Takarazuka Kinen. Ridden by [Norihiro Yokoyama](/wiki/Norihiro_Yokoyama "Norihiro Yokoyama"), he started the 17/10 favourite in a field of twelve which included Gentildonna, [Meisho Mambo](/wiki/Meisho_Mambo "Meisho Mambo"), [Tosen Jordan](/w/index.php?title=Tosen_Jordan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tosen Jordan (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%88%E3%83%BC%E3%82%BB%E3%83%B3%E3%82%B8%E3%83%A7%E3%83%BC%E3%83%80%E3%83%B3 "ja:トーセンジョーダン")], [Verxina](/wiki/Verxina "Verxina"), [Denim And Ruby](/w/index.php?title=Denim_And_Ruby&action=edit&redlink=1 "Denim And Ruby (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%87%E3%83%8B%E3%83%A0%E3%82%A2%E3%83%B3%E3%83%89%E3%83%AB%E3%83%93%E3%83%BC "ja:デニムアンドルビー")], and Win Variation. Gold Ship started poorly but recovered well and raced behind the leaders on the outside before taking the lead in the straight. He drew away in the closing stages to win by three lengths from the 55/1 outsider [Curren Mirotic](/w/index.php?title=Curren_Mirotic&action=edit&redlink=1 "Curren Mirotic (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%AB%E3%83%AC%E3%83%B3%E3%83%9F%E3%83%AD%E3%83%86%E3%82%A3%E3%83%83%E3%82%AF "ja:カレンミロティック")]. After the race, Yokoyama said, "He's a clever horse and whether he performs well or not depends on his mood. So I'm relieved that he lived up to the expectations of many fans".[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Gold Ship was then aimed at the [Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe](/wiki/Prix_de_l%27Arc_de_Triomphe "Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe") in France and began his preparation for the race with a run in the Grade 2 [Sapporo Kinen](/wiki/Sapporo_Kinen "Sapporo Kinen") over ten furlongs at [Sapporo Racecourse](/wiki/Sapporo_Racecourse "Sapporo Racecourse") on 24 August. He finished second, beaten three-quarters of a length by [Oka Shō](/w/index.php?title=Oka_Sh%C5%8D&action=edit&redlink=1 "Oka Shō (page does not exist)") winner [Harp Star](/wiki/Harp_Star "Harp Star"). Sugai said that it was "no disgrace to fail" when attempting to concede eleven pounds to the three-year-old filly.[[17]](#cite_note-17) In the Arc de Triomphe on 5 October, Gold Ship started at odds of 12.9/1 in a three-horse Japanese challenge which also included Harp Star and [Just A Way](/wiki/Just_A_Way "Just A Way"). After becoming agitated in the pre-race parade, he started poorly and raced at the back of the twenty-runner field for most of the way. He made some progress in the straight but never looked likely to make a serious challenge and finished fourteenth of the twenty runners, seven lengths behind the winner, [Treve](/wiki/Treve_(horse) "Treve (horse)"). Shortly after his return from Europe, Gold Ship topped the poll to decide the runners for the Arima Kinen. At Nakayama on 28 December he started the favourite for the Arima Kinen ahead of [Epiphaneia](/wiki/Epiphaneia_(horse) "Epiphaneia (horse)"), Just A Way and Gentildonna. He made steady progress on the outside in the straight but was unable to reach the leaders and finished third, beaten three quarters of a length and a nose by Gentildonna and [To The World](/w/index.php?title=To_The_World_(horse)&action=edit&redlink=1 "To The World (horse) (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%88%E3%82%A5%E3%82%B6%E3%83%AF%E3%83%BC%E3%83%AB%E3%83%89 "ja:トゥザワールド")].

### 2015: six-year-old season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Ship&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2015: six-year-old season")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Gold_Ship_Takarazuka_kinen_2015.jpg/250px-Gold_Ship_Takarazuka_kinen_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gold_Ship_Takarazuka_kinen_2015.jpg)

Gold Ship at the 2015 Takarazuka Kinen

On his first appearance of the new year, Gold Ship produced what the *Racing Post* described as a "dismal display"[[18]](#cite_note-18) when finishing unplaced in the [American Jockey Club Cup](/wiki/American_Jockey_Club_Cup "American Jockey Club Cup") at Nakayama on 25 January. On 23 March, Gold Ship recorded his 2015 campaign by winning the Hanshin Daishoten for the third consecutive time. On 4 May, he made his third attempt to win the spring edition of the Tennō Shō and started second favourite behind [Kizuna](/wiki/Kizuna_(horse) "Kizuna (horse)") in a seventeen-runner field. He started slowly and was towards the rear for the first half of the race, but made rapid progress on the back straight and turned for home in fourth place. Curren Mirotic opened up a clear lead, but Gold Ship produced a sustained run to take the lead in the final strides and won by a neck from the fast-finishing [Fame Game](/w/index.php?title=Fame_Game_(horse)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fame Game (horse) (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A7%E3%82%A4%E3%83%A0%E3%82%B2%E3%83%BC%E3%83%A0 "ja:フェイムゲーム")]. After the race Yokoyama said "I was thinking of going front if he started well, which obviously was not the case. From there on, I just concentrated on following the pace and careful not to turn him off. In the straight, he really showed his stamina and perseverance".[[19]](#cite_note-19) On 28 June, the grey started 9/10 favourite as he attempted a record third win in the Takarazuka Kinen. In what would later be referred to as the "12 billion yen incident", Gold Ship appeared to panic in the starting stalls. He lost many lengths at the start and finished fifteenth of the sixteen runners behind [Lovely Day](/wiki/Lovely_Day_(horse) "Lovely Day (horse)"). This is estimated to have lost 12 billion yen ($135 million) in betting tickets.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Yokoyama commented "I'm not sure what had happened in his mind because he can't tell us... he is just Gold Ship".[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Gold Ship returned in the autumn of 2015 for two more races before retirement. On 29 November he started the 3.7/1 second favourite for the Japan Cup. He finished tenth but was only three lengths behind the winner [Shonan Pandora](/wiki/Shonan_Pandora "Shonan Pandora"). He ended his racing career by running for the fourth time in the Arima Kinen on 27 December. He was made 3.1/1 favourite but finished eighth of the sixteen runners behind [Gold Actor](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Actor&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gold Actor (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%B4%E3%83%BC%E3%83%AB%E3%83%89%E3%82%A2%E3%82%AF%E3%82%BF%E3%83%BC "ja:ゴールドアクター")]. Gold Ship's retirement ceremony followed the race.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Assessment and awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Ship&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Assessment and awards")]

Following his win in the 2012 Arima Kinen, the *Racing Post* rated Gold Ship the best three-year-old in the world, one pound ahead of [Camelot](/wiki/Camelot_(horse) "Camelot (horse)"), [I'll Have Another](/wiki/I%27ll_Have_Another "I'll Have Another"), and [Dullahan](/wiki/Dullahan_(horse) "Dullahan (horse)").[[56]](#cite_note-56) Although he finished a distant second to the [filly](/wiki/Filly "Filly") Gentildonna in the vote for [Japanese Horse of the Year](/wiki/Japanese_Horse_of_the_Year "Japanese Horse of the Year"), he was the unanimous winner of the [JRA Award for Best Three-Year-Old Colt](/wiki/JRA_Award_for_Best_Three-Year-Old_Colt "JRA Award for Best Three-Year-Old Colt").[[57]](#cite_note-57) In the [2012 edition of the World Thoroughbred Racehorse Rankings](/wiki/2012_World_Thoroughbred_Rankings "2012 World Thoroughbred Rankings") Gold Ship was rated the second-best three-year-old colt, one pound behind I'll Have Another, and the thirteenth best horse in the world.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In 2013, Gold Ship was rated the eleventh-best racehorse in the world in the [World's Best Racehorse Rankings](/wiki/2013_World%27s_Best_Racehorse_Rankings "2013 World's Best Racehorse Rankings") and the equal second best four-year-old behind [Novellist](/wiki/Novellist_(horse) "Novellist (horse)") and level with [Declaration of War](/wiki/Declaration_of_War_(horse) "Declaration of War (horse)").[[59]](#cite_note-59) He was again rated eleventh in the 2014 edition of the rankings.

In the JRA Awards for 2014, Gold Ship finished third to Just A Way and Epiphaneia in the poll for the [JRA Award for Best Older Male Horse](/wiki/JRA_Award_for_Best_Older_Male_Horse "JRA Award for Best Older Male Horse").[[60]](#cite_note-60)

## Stud record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Ship&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Stud record")]

As of 2026, Gold Ship stands at Big Red Farm in [Niikappu, Hokkaido](/wiki/Niikappu,_Hokkaido "Niikappu, Hokkaido") at a stud fee of [JPY](/wiki/Japanese_yen "Japanese yen") 5,000,000.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Ship&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

Main article: [Gold Ship (*Umamusume: Pretty Derby*)](/wiki/Gold_Ship_(Umamusume:_Pretty_Derby) "Gold Ship (Umamusume: Pretty Derby)")

Gold Ship is heavily featured in the multimedia franchise *[Umamusume: Pretty Derby](/wiki/Umamusume:_Pretty_Derby "Umamusume: Pretty Derby")*, [anthropomorphized](/wiki/Moe_anthropomorphism "Moe anthropomorphism") as a tall, silver-haired teenage girl or young woman [of the same name](/wiki/Gold_Ship_(Umamusume:_Pretty_Derby) "Gold Ship (Umamusume: Pretty Derby)"), with a mischievous streak, voiced by [Hitomi Ueda](/wiki/Hitomi_Ueda "Hitomi Ueda"). She appears both as a recruitable trainee in the game and as one of the main characters in the [anime](/wiki/Umamusume:_Pretty_Derby#Anime_adaptation "Umamusume: Pretty Derby"),[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) as well as being the main host of the series's official [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") channel (which began activities in 2018). The series' popularity helped reignite public interest in the featured racehorses, with Gold Ship in particular attracting heavy interest; He came in 5th place at the "2021 [Net Buzz Word of the Year](/wiki/Internet_Buzzword_Award "Internet Buzzword Award")" while his *Uma Musume* counterpart came in 2nd place.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) In addition, a comedy [manga](/wiki/Manga "Manga") spinoff featuring the character titled *Uma Musume Pretty Derby PisuPisu☆SupiSupi Golshi-chan* launched on *[CoroCoro Comic](/wiki/CoroCoro_Comic "CoroCoro Comic")'*s website in November 2023.[[66]](#cite_note-66)
